annotations.label: Computable function (informal)

anchor.snippet: a function is computable if there is an algorithm that computes the value of the function

annotations.note: `Computable f` defines a total function as computable iff it is partial recursive, the formal counterpart of this informal notion.

annotations.label: Church–Turing thesis (lead)

anchor.snippet: The Church–Turing thesis is the unprovable assertion that every notion of computability

annotations.note: An informal/unprovable thesis, not a mathematical statement formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Function problem

anchor.snippet: the problem of computing the value of a function is known as a function problem

annotations.note: The complexity-theory notion of a function problem is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Computable function (formal)

anchor.snippet: is computable if there is an effective procedure that, given any

annotations.note: `Computable f := Partrec (f : α →. σ)` is exactly the formal definition of a computable function between `Primcodable` types.

annotations.label: μ-recursive functions

anchor.snippet: one can formalize computable functions as μ-recursive functions

annotations.note: The inductive `Nat.Partrec` is built from zero/succ/projections/pairing/composition/primitive recursion and `rfind` (unbounded minimization), i.e. the μ-recursive functions.

annotations.label: Calculability via a program

anchor.snippet: can be calculated if there exists a computer program with the following properties

annotations.note: The program-based characterization corresponds to `Computable`, which Mathlib also proves equivalent to Turing-machine computation elsewhere.

annotations.label: Basic characteristic of a computable function

anchor.snippet: The basic characteristic of a computable function is that there must be a finite procedure

annotations.note: The 'finite procedure' characterization is captured by `Computable` via partial recursive functions.

annotations.label: Summary characterization of computability

anchor.snippet: based on this view a function is computable if

annotations.note: This restated summary of computability is the `Computable` predicate.

annotations.label: Computable set

anchor.snippet: A set A of natural numbers is called computable

annotations.note: `ComputablePred p` (computable indicator function) formalizes a computable set, generalized from ℕ to any `Primcodable` type.

annotations.label: Computably enumerable set

anchor.snippet: A set of natural numbers is called computably enumerable

annotations.note: `REPred p` is defined as the domain of a partial computable function, the standard formalization of a c.e. set, generalized to `Primcodable` types.

annotations.label: Equivalent characterizations of c.e. sets

anchor.snippet: the following are equivalent for a nonempty subset B of the natural numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has the domain-of-partial-recursive characterization (`REPred`/`Partrec.dom_re`) but not the full list of equivalent conditions (range of a computable function, etc.).

annotations.label: Computable and c.e. relations

anchor.snippet: the notions of computable relation and computably enumerable relation can be defined from their analogues for sets

annotations.note: Relations can be expressed as `ComputablePred`/`REPred` over product `Primcodable` types, but there is no dedicated computable/c.e. relation definition.

annotations.label: Alphabet

anchor.snippet: An alphabet is an arbitrary set

annotations.note: The alphabet appears only as the implicit type parameter `α` of `Language α`; there is no standalone 'alphabet' declaration.

annotations.label: Word

anchor.snippet: A word on an alphabet is a finite sequence of symbols from the alphabet

annotations.note: Words are represented as `List α` (a core type used by `Language`), not by a dedicated Mathlib 'word' declaration.

annotations.label: Language

anchor.snippet: A language is a subset of the collection of all words on a fixed alphabet

annotations.note: `Language α := Set (List α)` is exactly a set of words over an alphabet.

annotations.label: Computable language

anchor.snippet: A language is called computable

annotations.note: A computable language would be `ComputablePred` over `List α`, but Mathlib has no specific 'computable language' definition tying this to `Language`.

annotations.label: Computably enumerable language

anchor.snippet: A language is computably enumerable

annotations.note: A c.e. language would be `REPred` over `List α`, but no dedicated definition connects this to the `Language` type.

annotations.label: Examples of computable functions

anchor.snippet: The following functions are computable

annotations.note: Basic examples like successor, addition and multiplication are proven primitive recursive (`Nat.Primrec.succ/add/mul`), hence computable.

annotations.label: Closure of computable functions

anchor.snippet: If f and g are computable, then so are

annotations.note: `Computable.comp` (with companion closure lemmas) establishes that computable functions are closed under composition.

annotations.label: Runs of fives in π

anchor.snippet: if there is a sequence of at least n consecutive fives in the decimal expansion of π

annotations.note: This specific illustrative example about decimal runs in π is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite segment of an uncomputable sequence

anchor.snippet: Each finite segment of an un computable sequence of natural numbers

annotations.note: This illustrative remark about finite segments being computable is not stated as a result in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Church–Turing thesis (statement)

anchor.snippet: any function computable from a procedure possessing the three properties listed above is a computable function

annotations.note: The Church–Turing thesis is an informal claim, not a formalizable theorem.

annotations.label: Provably total function

anchor.snippet: A function that can be proven to be computable is called provably total

annotations.note: The metamathematical notion of a provably total function relative to a proof system is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Provably total functions are r.e.

anchor.snippet: The set of provably total functions is recursively enumerable

annotations.note: This metatheorem about a proof system's provably total functions is not formalized.

annotations.label: Recursive definition

anchor.snippet: In a function defined by a recursive definition , each value is defined by a fixed first-order formula

annotations.note: Mathlib uses well-founded/structural recursion pervasively but has no decl capturing this descriptive meta-notion of 'recursive definition'.

annotations.label: Ackermann function

anchor.snippet: the Ackermann function , which is recursively defined but not primitive recursive

annotations.note: `ack` defines the Ackermann function and `not_primrec₂_ack`/`computable₂_ack` prove it is computable but not primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Well-ordered recursive functions are computable

anchor.snippet: Any function defined recursively in a well-ordered way is computable

annotations.note: No general Mathlib theorem states that functions defined by well-ordered recursion are computable.

annotations.label: Total functions not provably total exist

anchor.section: Total functions that are not provably total
anchor.snippet: the existence of total functions that cannot be proven total in the proof system

annotations.note: This metalogical existence result about provability of totality is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Countably many computable functions

anchor.snippet: there are only countably many computable functions

annotations.note: `Code` is `Denumerable`, giving countably many partial recursive programs, but Mathlib has no direct theorem stating the set of computable functions is countable.

annotations.label: Most real numbers are not computable

anchor.snippet: The real numbers are uncountable so most real numbers are not computable

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks a notion of computable real number, so this statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: Most subsets of naturals not computable

anchor.snippet: most subsets of the natural numbers are not computable

annotations.note: There is no Mathlib theorem comparing the cardinalities of computable versus all subsets of ℕ.

annotations.label: Entscheidungsproblem is undecidable

anchor.snippet: Turing and Church independently showed in the 1930s that this set of natural numbers is not computable

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the closely related halting problem is undecidable (`ComputablePred.halting_problem`, plus `rice`), but not the undecidability of first-order validity itself.

annotations.label: Computability relative to A

anchor.snippet: A function f is defined to be computable in A

annotations.note: `ComputableIn O f` (built on `Nat.RecursiveIn`) defines computability relative to oracles, generalizing computability relative to a single set A.

annotations.label: Hyperarithmetical theory

anchor.snippet: Hyperarithmetical theory studies those sets that can be computed from a computable ordinal number

annotations.note: Hyperarithmetical theory and computation from computable ordinals are not developed in Mathlib.
